title: Why Some Storms Produce Hail the Size of Softballs
summary: Softball-sized hail forms when supercell thunderstorms suspend ice in powerful updrafts long enough for repeated layers of supercooled water to freeze onto it.

lesson.learn: Giant hail is a sign that a thunderstorm has built a powerful vertical engine. Hail begins with a small ice embryo such as a frozen raindrop, graupel pellet, or bit of ice. Inside a severe storm, especially a supercell, strong updrafts lift that embryo through regions filled with supercooled liquid water: droplets that remain liquid below freezing. When those droplets hit the ice, they freeze onto it. If the updraft is strong and broad enough, the hailstone stays aloft instead of falling out. It may cycle through different temperature and moisture zones, collecting clear or cloudy layers depending on how quickly water freezes and how much air gets trapped. Softball-sized hail requires an unusually favorable balance: intense lift, abundant supercooled water, cold upper levels, and storm structure that keeps the growing stone in the growth zone. Eventually gravity wins. The hailstone falls when it becomes too heavy or exits the updraft, striking the ground at dangerous speeds.
lesson.simpler: Huge hail forms when a strong storm updraft keeps ice floating inside the cloud while supercooled droplets freeze onto it again and again. The bigger the updraft and growth zone, the larger the hail can get.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Softball-sized hail needs a rare combination of strong sustained updrafts, supercooled water, ice embryos, and storm organization that keeps stones growing before they fall.
lesson.deeperDive: Supercells are especially good hail factories because wind shear separates the updraft from the downdraft and lets the storm persist for hours. A rotating updraft can be both strong and organized, creating a protected region where hail embryos avoid being swept out too quickly. Growth happens by accretion: supercooled droplets collide with the ice and freeze. Wet growth can produce clearer layers when water spreads before freezing; dry growth traps more air and looks cloudy. Very large hail does not simply bounce up and down like a cartoon elevator. Modern research emphasizes paths through complex storm airflow, including broad updrafts, recycling zones, and interactions with melting, shedding, and collisions. The Vivian, South Dakota hailstone of July 23, 2010 measured 8 inches across, a reminder that rare storms can build ice far beyond ordinary hail size.
lesson.funFact: The U.S. record hailstone from Vivian, South Dakota was about the size of a volleyball.

references.title: NOAA NSSL: Severe Weather 101 - Hail Forecasting

people: Charles Knight - scientist known for major hailstone research
Leslie Lemon - meteorologist associated with supercell storm structure studies
places: Vivian, South Dakota - site of the 2010 U.S. record hailstone
Great Plains - region where supercells often produce large hail
years: 2010 - Vivian, South Dakota hailstone set a U.S. size record
1970s - supercell structure research improved severe-storm understanding
concepts: Supercell - long-lived thunderstorm with a rotating updraft
Updraft - rising air current that supports hail growth
Supercooled water - liquid droplets below freezing temperature
Accretion - growth by droplets freezing onto an ice particle
